text: The video shows a nodule causing bowel-on-bowel syndrome, with the sigmoid colon visibly plastered over the anterior aspect of the rectum due to fibrosis introduced by the nodule.
The initial bowel dissection is completed on both sides, with visible separation of the bowel from surrounding adhesions.
The surgeon opens the pararectal spaces on both sides, creating a large pararectal window visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs dissection at the pararectal or parasigmoidal fat, ensuring the lumen of the bowel is protected during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to perform adhesiolysis, focusing on the fat that forms adhesions. The procedure is conducted without a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon approaches the anterior aspect of the rectum, exposing the anterior lateral aspect to facilitate access to the bowel. The sigmoid colon appears coiled, with nodular growth causing impingement on the posterior side.
The surgeon applies counter traction using laparoscopic instruments, palpating a nodule with a stone-like consistency through haptic feedback.
The surgeon attempts to cut through the stone-like consistency of the tissue to expose the nodule, facilitating complete dissection of the nodular growth.
The surgeon cuts through the nodule while providing cranial traction to the uterus using a needle holder.

The surgeon cuts through a stony hard nodule using a needle holder to provide cranial traction to the uterus. The nodule's hard consistency causes difficulty in traction and results in coiling over itself due to nodularity in the para rectal margins.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the anterior aspect of the rectum after cutting through the nodule. The remaining length of the rectum from the anorectal junction is assessed visually to ensure adequate length for bowel resection and to maintain continence post-anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the bowel region below the fibrosed nodule, which has caused dilatation of the bowel above it. The dissection proceeds by separating the bowel from the Denonvilliers fascia, revealing approximately nine centimeters of bowel length remaining below the nodule.

The surgeon uses suction to separate the nodule from its posterior vaginal attachment, exposing the pararectal fossa. The Bisinger bipolar instrument is employed for precise dissection, revealing a clear tissue plane.
The surgeon has obtained a clear dissection plane in the pelvic region. No irrigation is used to avoid the prismatic effect, maintaining a dry surgical field.

The surgeon dissects and releases the native tissue plane to separate the anterolateral and anterior aspects of the rectum from their attachments up to the level of the perineal body. A similar dissection is attempted on the opposite side.
The surgeon navigates the anterolateral plane to approach the anterior aspect of the rectum, making a bold incision through the margin to ensure safety on the serosal aspect of the rectum.

The surgeon makes a bold cut through the anterior margin, releasing a significant length of the bowel. The large nodularity is clearly visible on the bowel, and the bowel is liberated from the fibrotic cluster induced by the nodule in the pararectal region.

The surgeon has dissected the entire bowel through its length up to the perineal body on the anterior side, revealing a large nodule causing puckering in the bowel region. The bowel appears unhealthy, with visible signs of nodularity and puckering.

The surgeon begins preparing the distal stump for anastomosis, exposing the pararectal fat. The exposure is limited to approximately 0.5 centimeters to preserve vascularity. The anastomotic site is prepared circumferentially by sequentially removing the pararectal fat, ensuring excellent capillary and vascularity.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to perform circumferential dissection, revealing the lumen of the healthy rectum. This visual confirmation aids in preparing the anastomotic site effectively.

The surgeon prepares the distal stump and passes the linear Endo GIA stapler, ensuring the bowel is squeezed inside the stapler blades without including other structures like the ureter. The port is widened, and the stapler fires across the prepared bowel.

The surgeon ensures the bowel is positioned correctly before firing the linear stapler, achieving a clean cut. The bowel may require up to two linear cuts, but typically, the bowel size in Indian women is smaller, necessitating fewer cuts.

The linear endo GI stapler is fired, creating a clean linear cut in three rows through the distal stump of the bowel. The stapler is then removed, and the remaining stump is refashioned using scissors to ensure no interference with the surgical site.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic procedure on the proximal stump, inserting a bowel clamp through a 5mm port. A small suprapubic incision is made to exteriorize the bowel for resection. Scissors are used to cut the bowel, with the process clearly visible.
The surgeon opens the lumen on the proximal end of the bowel, ensuring a clean cut through the tissue. The next step involves fixing the anvil inside the bowel.

The surgeon makes a small suprapubic incision, approximately three centimeters, to insert the anvil for the circular stapler into the peritoneal cavity. A purse string suture is tied using polyglactin (vicryl) to secure the anvil, ensuring it is snug to prevent the escape of the tissue donut through the circular stapler.

The surgeon passes the circular stapler through the anus, positioning it on the anterior aspect of the distal stump to perform an end-to-side anastomosis. This technique is chosen for optimal alignment and healing, utilizing the available length of the bowel.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side anastomosis using a circular stapler, with the pin entering through the long distal stump of the bowel, ensuring a good length for the anastomosis.

The surgeon fits the anvil through the bowel, preparing the circular stapler for firing. The orange mark on the stapler indicates readiness to fire, ensuring the creation of uniform tissue donuts. These donuts are visually inspected and prepared for histopathological examination, especially in cases of malignancy or endometriosis.

The surgeon fits the anvil onto the opposite end of the circular stapler, verifying the alignment through all edges. The orange mark on the stapler, indicating readiness for firing, becomes visible as the long distal stump is dissected open.
The surgeon ensures the anastomotic site is well vascularized before firing the circular stapler to complete the anastomosis. The stapler is positioned at the site, ready to be activated.
The surgeon dissects the mucosal aspect around the orange mark, which is visible on the circular stapler, ensuring precise alignment for the anastomosis.
The surgeon ensures the anti-mesenteric to anti-mesenteric border of the colon is aligned to prevent twisting before firing the circular stapler.

The circular stapler is fired and held in place for ten seconds before being gently released and removed through the anal canal. The uterus is visible and intact, indicating a conservative approach. The stapler is opened from the inside and removed through the distal stump, revealing a successful anastomosis confirmed by the presence of intact donuts.
The video shows the completed end-to-side anastomosis with visible staple lines on both ends. The anvil is present with both donuts, indicating a successful anastomosis.
